perturbation.original_text: During the Term of this Agreement, Premier shall be required to purchase a Minimum Annual Order Volume ("MAOV") of [***] ("Units") for the twelve-month period commencing July 1, 2018 and ending June 30, 2019.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term of this Agreement, Premier shall be required to purchase a Minimum Annual Order Volume ("MAOV") of [***] ("Units") for the twelve-month period commencing July 1, 2018 and ending June 30, 2019. Any disputes regarding this purchase must be resolved within 30 days of initial claim.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of a 30-day dispute resolution deadline may be too short, especially if legal action is required, conflicting with general statutes of limitations for contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. The modified text imposes a 30-day dispute resolution deadline, which is far shorter and could prevent a party from pursuing their legal rights under the contract if a resolution is not reached within that limited timeframe, effectively contradicting the statutory limitation period.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 30-day dispute resolution deadline, which directly contradicts the four-year statute of limitations for written contracts as stated in California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The added clause limits the time available to pursue legal action, effectively undermining the statutory protection provided by the law.

perturbation.original_text: The MAOV [***] Units for each twelve-month period commencing July 1, 2019 through the end of the Term, contingent upon commercial aseptic production at Heritage's [***] facility and approval of that facility by Premier by January 1, 2019.
perturbation.changed_text: The MAOV [***] Units for each twelve-month period commencing July 1, 2019 through the end of the Term, contingent upon commercial aseptic production at Heritage's [***] facility and approval of that facility by Premier by January 1, 2019. Any claim for breach of this agreement must be initiated within 6 months from the date of the alleged breach.
perturbation.explanation: The contract specifies a requirement for facility approval by January 1, 2019. Introducing a 6-month limit to file a breach claim is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 sets a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract. By stipulating that any breach of contract claim must be initiated within six months of the alleged breach, the modified text directly contradicts the legally established statute of limitations.

(b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 6-month limitation period for breach of contract claims, directly contradicting California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, which provides a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon any contract, obligation, or liability founded upon an instrument in writing as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: '337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing'. This makes the contractual term unenforceable as it violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: For the avoidance of doubt, the six-month period commencing July 1, 2022 and ending December 31, 2022, Premier will be required to purchase [***] (the twelve-month periods and the six-month period are each a "Contract Period").
perturbation.changed_text: For the avoidance of doubt, the six-month period commencing July 1, 2022 and ending December 31, 2022, Premier will be required to purchase [***] (the twelve-month periods and the six-month period are each a "Contract Period"). Premier will be liable for any damages if not purchased within the contract period, with Heritage holding no duty to mitigate damages.
perturbation.explanation: The additional clause eliminates Heritage's duty to mitigate damages, potentially conflicting with contract law principles requiring reasonable efforts to minimize losses.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3353 requires damages for breach of contract to align with the detriment proximately caused, which can be interpreted to require mitigation. By stating there is 'no duty to mitigate,' the changed text contradicts the general principle that the non-breaching party must take reasonable steps to reduce damages, and the damages need to proximately result from the breach itself. Limiting damages and causation are forms of mitigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3353. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 4. General Provisions [3353 - 3361] ( Article 4 enacted 1872. ) 3353. In estimating damages, the value of property to a seller thereof is deemed to be the price which he could have obtained therefor in the market nearest to the place at which it should have been accepted by the buyer, and at such time after the breach of the contract as would have sufficed, with reasonable diligence, for the seller to effect a resale. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established legal principles regarding the duty to mitigate damages in contract law, creating an inconsistency. The Perturbation Explanation states that the added clause eliminates Heritage's duty to mitigate damages. The Law Explanation indicates that California Civil Code § 3353 requires damages for breach of contract to align with the detriment proximately caused, which can be interpreted to require mitigation. The added clause stating 'no duty to mitigate' directly opposes the principle that the non-breaching party must take reasonable steps to reduce damages.
